verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And certain men which came down from Judea taught the brethren, and said, Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how at every step of the right progress in respect of the Gentiles, the beginning is brought in as matter of necessity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Before this (Peter) being found fault with, justified himself, and said all that he said in the tone of apology, which was what made his words acceptable: then, the Jews having turned away, upon this (Paul) came to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again, seeing another extravagance coming in, upon this (the apostle) enacts the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as it is likely that they, as being taught of God, discoursed to all indifferently, this moved to jealousy them of the Jews (who had believed).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they did not merely speak of circumcision, but they said, Ye cannot even be saved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas the very opposite to this was the case, that receiving circumcision they could not be saved.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how closely the trials succeed each other, from within, from without?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is well ordered too, that this happens when Paul is present, that he may answer them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small dissension and disputation with them, they determined that Paul and Barnabas, and certain other of them, should go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders about this question.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul does not say, What?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have I not a right to be believed after so many signs? but he complied for their sakes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And being brought on their way by the Church, they passed through Phenice and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles: and they caused great joy unto all the brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, the consequence is that all the Samaritans also, learn what has come to the Gentiles: and they rejoiced.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come to Jerusalem, they were received of the Church, and of the apostles and elders, and they declared all things that God had done with them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what a providence is here!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the apostles and elders came together to consider of this matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when there had been much disputing, Peter rose up and said unto them, Men and brethren, ye know how that of old days God made choice among us, that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the Gospel, and believe.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe Peter from the first standing aloof (κεχωρισμένον) from the affair, and even to this time judaizing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet (says he) “ye know.” (ch. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps those were present who of old found fault with him in the matter of Cornelius, and went in with him (on that occasion): for this reason he brings them forward as witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “From old days,” he says, “did choose among you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “Among you?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Either, in Palestine, or, you being present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “By my mouth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he shows that it was God speaking by him, and no human utterance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God, that knoweth the hearts, gave testimony unto them:” he refers them to the spiritual testimony: “by giving them the Holy Ghost even as unto us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Everywhere he puts the Gentiles upon a thorough equality.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And put no difference between us and them, having purified their hearts by faith.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From faith alone, he says, they obtained the same gifts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is also meant as a lesson to those (objectors); this is able to teach even them that faith only is needed, not works nor circumcision.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed they do not say all this only by way of apology for the Gentiles, but to teach (the Jewish believers) also to abandon the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, at present this is not said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now therefore why tempt ye God, to put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “Tempt ye God?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As if He had not power to save by faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consequently, it proceeds from a want of faith, this bringing in the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he shows that they themselves were nothing benefited by it, and he turns the whole (stress of his speech) against the Law, not against them, and (so) cuts short the accusation of them: “which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved, even as they.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How full of power these words!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same that Paul says at large in the Epistle to the Romans, the same says Peter here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For if Abraham,” says (Paul), “was justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you perceive that all this is more a lesson for them than apology for the Gentiles?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, if he had spoken this without a plea for speaking, he would have been suspected: an occasion having offered, he lays hold of it, and speaks out fearlessly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See on all occasions how the designs of their foes are made to work with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If those had not stirred the question, these things would not have been spoken, nor what follows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.) (b) But let us look more closely at what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And certain men,” etc. In Jerusalem, then, there were not any believers from among the Gentiles: but in Antioch of course there were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore there came down certain yet laboring under this disease of the love of rule, and wishing to have those of the Gentiles attached to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet Paul, though he too was learned in the Law, was not thus affected.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When therefore Paul and Barnabas had no small disputation with them,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he returned from thence, the doctrine also became more exact.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if they at Jerusalem enjoin no such thing, much more these (have no right to do so).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And being brought on their way,” etc, “they caused no small joy to the brethren.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark, as many as are not enamoured of rule, rejoiced in their believing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was no ambitious feeling that prompted their recitals, neither was it for display, but in justification of the preaching to the Gentiles. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus they say nothing of what had happened in the matter of the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there arose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5.) (a) But even if they would needs bring over the Gentiles to their side, they learn that neither must the Apostles overlook it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Apostles and elders,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Among us,” he says, “God chose:” and “from old days:” long ago, he says, not now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this too is no small point—at a time when Jews believed, not turned away (from the Gospel).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Among us;” an argument from the place: “of old days,” from the time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that expression, “Chose:” just as in their own case he says not, (so) willed it, but, “Chose that the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the word of the Gospel and believe.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence is this proved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he shows that the testimony given them is not of grace merely, but of their virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God which knoweth the hearts bare them witness” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8); having afforded to them nothing less (than to us), for, he says, “Put no difference between us and them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, hearts are what one must everywhere look to.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is very appositely said, “God that knoweth the hearts bare them witness:” as in the former instance, “Thou, Lord, that knowest the hearts of all men.” (ch. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to show that this is the meaning, observe what he adds, “Put no difference between us and them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he has mentioned the testimony borne to them, then he utters that great word, the same which Paul speaks, “Neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Cor. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “That he may make the twain one in Himself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of all these the seeds lie in Peter’s discourse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say (between) them of the circumcision, but “Between us,” that is the Apostles, “and them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, that the expression, “no difference” may not seem an outrage, After faith, he says—“Having purified their hearts by faith” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10)—He thoroughly cleansed them first.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he shows, not that the Law was evil, but themselves weak.—“But we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved even as they.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how he ends with a fearful consideration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not discourse to them from the Prophets, but from things present, of which themselves were witnesses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of course (the Prophets) also themselves anon add their testimony (infra v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15), and make the reason stronger by what has now come to pass.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, he first permits the question to be moved in the Church, and then speaks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And put no difference between”—he said not, them of the circumcision, but “us and them,” i.e. the Gentiles: for this (gradual advance) little by little is stronger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Why therefore tempt ye God?” who is become (the) God of the Gentiles: for this was tempting: * * * whether He is able to save even after the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what he does.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that they are in danger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, what the Law could not do, faith had power to do, “we believe that through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved even as they” (comp.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16): but faith falling off, behold, themselves (are) in destruction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he did not say, Why do ye disbelieve? which was more harsh, but, “Tempt God,” and that when the fact is demonstrated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (c) Great effrontery this, of the Pharisees, that even after faith they set up the Law, and will not obey the Apostles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But see these, how mildly they speak, and not in the tone of authority: such words are amiable, and more apt to fix themselves in the mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, it is nowhere a display of words, but demonstration by facts, by the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, though they have such proofs, they still speak gently.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe they do not come accusing those at Antioch, but “declaring all things that God had done with them:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4) but thence again these men lay hold upon the occasion (to compass their own objects), “but there rose up,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such were the pains they took in their love of power: and it was not with the knowledge of the Apostles that they Paul and Barnabas were blamed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But still they brought forward none of these charges: but when they have proved the matter, then (the Apostles) write in stronger terms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For gentleness is everywhere a great good: gentleness, I say, not stupid indifference; gentleness, not adulation: for between these there is a vast difference.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing ruffled Paul, nothing discomposed Peter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When thou hast convincing proofs, why lose thy temper, to render these of none effect?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is impossible for one who is out of temper ever to persuade.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yesterday also we discoursed about anger; but there is no reason why we should not to-day also; perchance a second exhortation coming directly after the first will effect somewhat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed a medicine though of virtue to heal a wound, unless it be constantly renewed, mars all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And think not that our continual discoursing about the same things is a condemning of you: for if we condemned you, we should not discourse; but now, hoping that you will gain much, we speak these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would indeed that we did speak constantly of the same things: would that there were no other subject of our discourses, than how we might overcome our passions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For is it not contrary to all reason, that while emperors, living in luxury and so great honor, have no subject of discourse either while sitting at table, or at any other time, save only how to overcome their enemies—and therefore it is that they hold their assemblies each day, and appoint generals and soldiers, and demand taxes and tributes; and that of all state affairs, the moving causes are these two, the overcoming of those who make war upon them, and the establishing of their subjects in peace—we have no mind for such themes as this, nor ever even dream of conversing upon them: but how we may buy land, or purchase slaves, and make our property greater, these are subjects we can talk about every day, and never be tired of them: while concerning things in ourselves and really our own, we neither wish to speak ourselves, nor so much as dream of tolerating advice, nor of enduring to hear others speaking about them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But answer me, what do you talk about?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: About dinner?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why that is a subject for cooks.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, that is a theme for hucksters and merchants.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of buildings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That belongs to carpenters and builders.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That talk is for husbandmen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But for us, there is no other proper business, save this, how we may make wealth for the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let not the discourse be wearisome to you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why is it that none finds fault with the physician for always discoursing of the healing art, nor with people of other crafts for talking about their peculiar arts?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If indeed the mastery over our passions were really achieved, so that there were no need of putting us in mind, we might reasonably be taxed with ambition and display: or rather, not then either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even if it were gained, for all that, there would be need of discoursing, that one might not relapse and remain uncorrected: as in fact physicians discourse not only to the sick, but also to the whole, and they have books on this subject, on the one part how to free from disease, on the other how to preserve health.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that even if we are well, still we must not give over, but must do all in order to the preserving of our health.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we are sick there is a twofold necessity for advice: first, that we may be freed from the disease; secondly, that having been freed, we may not fall into it again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, we are discoursing now by the method of treating the sick, not by the rules for the treatment of the healthy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then may one root out this evil passion? how subdue (ὑποσκελίσειε) this violent fever?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us see whence it had its birth, and let us remove the cause.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence is it wont to arise?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From arrogance and much haughtiness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This cause then let us remove, and the disease is removed together with it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what is arrogance? whence does it arise? for perhaps we are likely to have to go back to a still higher origin.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But whatever course the reason of the thing may point out, that let us take, that we may go to the bottom of the mischief, and pluck it up by the roots.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence then comes arrogance?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From our not looking into our own concerns, but instead of that, busying ourselves about the nature of land, though we are not husbandmen, and the nature of gold, though we are not merchants, and concerning clothing, and everything else: while to ourselves and our own nature we never look at all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And who, you will say, is ignorant of his own nature?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many: perhaps all, save a few: and if ye will, I will show the proof of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, tell me, what is man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If one were asked, will he be able to answer outright to the questions, In what he differs from the brutes, in what he is akin to the heavenly inhabitants, what can be made of man?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as in the case of any other material, so also in this case: man is the subject-matter, but of this can be made either an angel or a beast.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does not this seem a strange saying?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet ye have often heard it in the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of certain human beings it was said, “he is the angel of the Lord” (Mal. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7): and “from his lips,” saith it, “they shall seek judgment” (Mal. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1): and again, “I send My angel before Thy face:” but of some, “Serpents, generation of vipers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, it all depends upon the use.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do I say, an angel? the man can become God, and a child of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we read, “I have said, Ye are gods, and all of you are children of the Most High.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. lxxxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what is greater, the power to become both God and angel and child of God is put into his own hands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yea, so it is, man can be the maker of an angel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perchance this saying has startled you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear however Christ saying: “In the Resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like unto the angels.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, “He that is able to receive it, let him receive it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In a word, it is virtue which makes angels: but this is in our power: therefore we are able to make angels, though not in nature, certainly in will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed if virtue be absent, it is no advantage to be an angel by nature; and the Devil is a proof of this, who was an angel once: but if virtue be present, it is no loss to be a man by nature; and John is a proof of this, who was a man, and Elias who went up into heaven, and all those who are about to depart thither.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For these indeed, though with bodies, were not prevented from dwelling in heaven: while those others, though without bodies, could not remain in heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let no one then grieve or be vexed with his nature as if it were a hindrance to him, but with his will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He (the Devil) from being incorporeal became a lion: for lo! it saith, “Our adversary, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour” (1 Pet. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8): we from being corporeal, become angels.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as if a person, having found some precious material, should despise it, as not being an artificer, it will be a great loss to him, whether it be pearls, or a pearl shell, or any other such thing that he has seen; so we likewise, if we are ignorant of our own nature, shall despise it much: but if we know what it is, we shall exhibit much zeal, and reap the greatest profits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For from this nature is wrought a king’s robe, from this a king’s house, from this nature are fashioned a king’s members: all are kingly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not then misuse our own nature to our hurt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has made us “a little lower than the angels,” (Ps. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5), I mean, by reason of death: but even that little we have now recovered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is nothing therefore to hinder us from becoming nigh to the angels, if we will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then will it, let us will it, and having exercised ourselves thoroughly, let us return honor to the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit, now and ever, world without end, Amen.
